postData.contentHtml:
The New Zealand thoroughbred racing community is deeply saddened by the news of champion sire Savabeel's death at the age of 24. The iconic stallion, a cornerstone of Waikato Stud, suffered a fractured shoulder in a paddock accident, bringing an end to an extraordinary career that spanned both the racetrack and the breeding shed.

Savabeel, a magnificent son of the legendary Zabeel, carried on a revered lineage that also included his grandsire, Sir Tristram. His own racing career was marked by elite success, most notably a victory in the Group 1 Cox Plate as a three-year-old, alongside a win in the Spring Champion Stakes. He retired to stud in 2005 after a significant $10 million deal, embarking on what would become an unparalleled breeding journey.

Over the past two decades, Savabeel established himself as New Zealand's ten-time champion sire, leaving an undeniable impact on the quality of thoroughbreds. His progeny boast an astounding 159 stakes winners, including an impressive 36 Group 1 victors. Among his celebrated offspring are star mares such as Probabeel, Atishu, and Orchestral, as well as Group 1-winning colts like Mo’unga, Cool Aza Beel, and Noverre.

His influence extended beyond his direct progeny, with his daughters proving to be exceptional broodmares. They have produced 46 stakes winners and nine elite-level scorers, including the dual Group 1 winner Autumn Boy and Empire Rose Stakes winner Ice Bath. Even in his final season, Savabeel remained active, covering 88 mares, with plans for a reduced capacity in 2026.

Figures from Waikato Stud and those who knew him best have lauded his immense contribution. Waikato Stud's General Manager, Mike Rennie, described him as "what every stud on the face of the earth dreams to have," highlighting his fighting fit condition until the very end. Sales and Nominations Manager George Chittick remembered him as a "proper gentleman" and a "champion in Australasia," emphasizing the profound emotional impact his passing had on the entire team. His former trainer, Graeme Rogerson, reflected fondly on the "bright side and all of the good things that he brought us, and the great times we had with the horse.”

The outpouring of sentiment following his death has been predominantly positive, reflecting the widespread admiration and respect for his monumental contributions to horse racing in New Zealand and beyond. Savabeel's legacy will undoubtedly continue to shape the thoroughbred industry for generations to come, through his sons at stud and the remarkable producing capabilities of his daughters.
postData.title: An Enduring Legacy: New Zealand Bids Farewell to Champion Sire Savabeel
postData.summary: New Zealand's thoroughbred industry mourns the passing of Savabeel, the ten-time champion sire, who died at the age of 24 following a paddock accident. His remarkable career as a racehorse and an even more influential stallion has left an indelible mark on Australasian bloodstock.
